bins.name: Oppose April 2026 Deadline
bins.desc: The State Entities argue that the Secretary's April 30, 2026, deadline for final FERC action is too tight, warning it could limit meaningful public participation and weaken the legal durability of any resulting rules. They ask FERC to allow reasonable comment periods consistent with the APA and Section 403 of the DOE Organization Act.
quotes: Rushing this rulemaking process could substantially curtail meaningful public participation opportunities and detract from the legal durability of any final rules established as a result of this rulemaking process.
the State Entities encourage the Commission to provide reasonable time periods for stakeholders to provide comment and otherwise participate in this proceeding.

bins.name: Preserve State Distribution Authority
bins.desc: The State Entities note that FERC has not historically regulated load interconnections and that legal precedent for the claimed jurisdiction is thin. They support FERC clarifying a basis for jurisdiction over transmission interconnections but insist any rule be narrowly tailored to avoid encroaching on state authority over distribution and retail ratemaking. They also want the rule to leave room for states and RTOs to build on federal baselines.
quotes: there does not appear to be legal precedent directly granting this jurisdiction, even when limited to transmission interconnections.
the Commission must establish clear jurisdictional boundaries that appropriately respect state jurisdictional authority over the distribution system and retail ratemaking.

the Commission's rulemaking must be carefully designed and narrowly tailored to avoid an expansion of this jurisdiction over end-use customer loads interconnecting to the local electric distribution system.
the Commission should clearly define the scope of this rulemaking.
different regions across the country should be allowed to build upon federal standards to create rules that address unique regional challenges and opportunities.
any Commission rulemaking here should not impede that progress or prevent other regions from making similar progress.

bins.name: Full Cost Assignment to Large Loads
bins.desc: The filing endorses the ANOPR principle that large loads bear 100% of assigned network upgrade costs, and pushes FERC to extend that to all costs caused by large loads, both direct and indirect, so ratepayers do not subsidize interconnection.
quotes: the State Entities are encouraged by the ANOPR's inclusion of ratepayer protection principles, including that "[l]oad and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades they are assigned."

this rulemaking must include provisions that mitigate the risk of ratepayer subsidization of large load interconnection, including by ensuring that the above cost assignment principle includes a robust assessment that assigns all costs caused by large loads (both direct and indirect) to those same large load customers.

bins.name: Protect Against Project Abandonment
bins.desc: The State Entities call for financial protections that shield ratepayers if large load customers fail to materialize, abandon projects, or exit the system early, preventing stranded transmission costs from being socialized onto existing customers.
quotes: The Commission's rulemaking should also include protections that shield ratepayers from cost risk associated with large load customers that fail to materialize, abandon interconnecting projects, or prematurely exit the transmission system.

bins.name: Ratepayer Cost Protection
bins.desc: The filing makes cost protection the central ratepayer ask: full and direct cost assignment, including indirect costs, plus financial safeguards against abandonment. The filers stress that ratepayers already face rising energy costs and cannot absorb new burdens from large load interconnection.
quotes: the State Entities are encouraged by the ANOPR's inclusion of ratepayer protection principles, including that "[l]oad and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades they are assigned."

this rulemaking must include provisions that mitigate the risk of ratepayer subsidization of large load interconnection, including by ensuring that the above cost assignment principle includes a robust assessment that assigns all costs caused by large loads (both direct and indirect) to those same large load customers.

The Commission's rulemaking should also include protections that shield ratepayers from cost risk associated with large load customers that fail to materialize, abandon interconnecting projects, or prematurely exit the transmission system.

bins.name: Incentivize Load Flexibility
bins.desc: The State Entities support ANOPR language encouraging curtailability and dispatchability for large loads, arguing that demand response and load flexibility reduce peak demand strain and the size of costly transmission upgrades. They urge FERC to consider expanding the rulemaking to capture additional flexibility mechanisms.
quotes: the State Entities support efforts by the Commission to protect ratepayers by allowing and/or incentivizing large load customers to participate in curtailment, load flexibility, and other demand response mechanisms.
the Commission should consider expanding the scope of this rulemaking to consider additional load flexibility mechanisms which can similarly alleviate the peak demand burden on ratepayers.

bins.name: Demand Response and Curtailment Rights
bins.desc: The filing supports ANOPR provisions that allow system operators to curtail or dispatch large loads, and asks FERC to go further by incentivizing participation in a broader range of demand response programs that shift withdrawals away from peak hours.
quotes: the State Entities support efforts by the Commission to protect ratepayers by allowing and/or incentivizing large load customers to participate in curtailment, load flexibility, and other demand response mechanisms.
the Commission should consider expanding the scope of this rulemaking to consider additional load flexibility mechanisms which can similarly alleviate the peak demand burden on ratepayers.

bins.name: Preserve State and RTO Rules
bins.desc: Many states and RTOs have already begun developing large load interconnection tariffs. The State Entities want any federal baseline to leave room for these existing and future regional frameworks rather than preempting or impeding them.
quotes: different regions across the country should be allowed to build upon federal standards to create rules that address unique regional challenges and opportunities.
any Commission rulemaking here should not impede that progress or prevent other regions from making similar progress.
